notes.note: L. Calpurnius Piso Caesoninus (consul 58) was Caesar's father-in-law, his daughter Calpurnia being Caesar's wife; Cicero expects Piso's disapproval of his son-in-law's course to sting Caesar.

notes.anchor: born not of a quarrel between citizens but of the audacity of one ruined citizen
notes.note: Cicero draws a careful distinction: a true civil war springs from a faction-quarrel within the citizen body, whereas this conflict (he charges) arose solely from the recklessness of Caesar, a single bankrupt citizen turning his army on the state.

notes.anchor: not even Picenum was a known country
notes.note: Picenum, the Adriatic region of east-central Italy, was traditionally Pompeian territory (his family's heartland); Cicero's gibe is that Pompey has so mismanaged the campaign that he does not even know the ground in his own stronghold.

notes.anchor: All his hope rests on two legions
notes.note: The two legions were those recalled from Caesar in 50 BC on the pretext of a Parthian war and handed to Pompey; Cicero suspects their loyalty ("almost not his own") because they had so recently served under Caesar.

notes.anchor: best to send them off to Greece
notes.note: Cicero weighs evacuating his son and nephew to Greece, away from the fighting in Italy and toward the safety of Atticus's connections in Epirus and Athens.

notes.anchor: when Dolabella comes to mind, I breathe a little
notes.note: P. Cornelius Dolabella, Tullia's husband, was a partisan of Caesar; his standing in the Caesarian camp gave Cicero some reassurance that Tullia and Terentia might be safe even if Rome fell to Caesar.

notes.anchor: it is more obscure than the number of Plato
notes.note: A byword for impenetrable obscurity: the "nuptial number" of Plato's Republic (8.546) was already proverbially baffling in antiquity. Cicero says Atticus's riddle of the Oppii is harder still.
